title: Sudden Blindness and Dilated Pupils in Cats: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Sudden blindness and dilated pupils in cats are signs of a medical emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is critical for your cat's health.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden blindness and dilated pupils are critical veterinary emergencies that require immediate medical attention to potentially restore vision and treat serious underlying diseases.

What causes sudden blindness and dilated pupils in cats?

Sudden blindness, or acute vision loss, often presents with pupils that remain wide (dilated) regardless of light levels. This condition, known as mydriasis, indicates that the eye or the brain is not properly processing visual stimuli. Common causes include feline hypertension (high blood pressure), retinal detachment, or neurological trauma.

Is sudden blindness in my cat a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Sudden vision loss is a true veterinary emergency.
  • Time is of the essence; for conditions like retinal detachment caused by high blood pressure, immediate treatment may sometimes restore vision.
  • If left untreated, the damage is often permanent and can indicate severe underlying systemic disease.

How does a photo of my cat’s eyes help with the triage process?

  • Providing a high-quality photo to a veterinary professional can significantly speed up the triage process.
  • A photo allows the medical team to check for internal bleeding and assess the symmetry of the pupils.
  • The visual data helps identify physical changes in the lens or retina, ensuring your cat receives the appropriate level of urgent care upon arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with sudden blindness and dilated pupils, potential diagnoses include retinal detachment secondary to systemic hypertension or intraocular hemorrhage, and intracranial diseases such as pituitary or paranasal masses, meningiomas, or lymphosarcoma affecting the optic chiasm. Central blindness involving the optic tracts or occipital cortex can also manifest with acute blindness but with normal pupillary light reflexes; etiologies include cardiac arrest during anesthesia, seizures, or severe head trauma. Rapid diagnosis and aggressive therapy are essential for maintenance of vision.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1700)

faqs.question: What are common signs of sudden blindness in cats?
faqs.answer: Besides dilated pupils, you may notice your cat bumping into furniture, hesitating to jump, or acting suddenly disoriented and vocalizing more than usual.

faqs.question: Can high blood pressure be the cause?
faqs.answer: Yes, systemic hypertension is one of the most common causes of sudden blindness in older cats, often related to kidney disease or hyperthyroidism.

faqs.question: Is sudden blindness painful for cats?
faqs.answer: While the blindness itself might not be painful, the underlying causes, such as glaucoma or high blood pressure, can cause significant discomfort or headaches.
